findings.title: No API authentication on PM server endpoints
findings.description: The PM server exposes several HTTP endpoints (e.g., GET /api/pipeline/:runId/tasks, /api/pipeline/:runId/issues, /api/pipeline/:runId/pr, POST /api/pipeline/intake) but no task in the plan addresses authentication or authorization for these endpoints. Any network-reachable client could trigger pipeline runs, read issue data with assignee information, or access PR metadata. The ingress in Task 9 adds rate limiting but not authentication.
findings.recommendation: Add a task to implement API authentication on the PM server — at minimum API key authentication via an Authorization header validated in Elysia middleware. For the frontend, use a session-based or token-based auth flow. Define an auth middleware subtask under Task 2 or as a new task, and ensure Task 9 ingress annotations do not bypass it.

findings.title: GitHub PAT with broad repo scope stored as static secret
findings.description: Task 1 provisions a GitHub Personal Access Token with 'repo scope' as a sealed secret. PATs with full repo scope grant read/write access to all repositories the token owner can access, not just 5dlabs/sigma-1. If this secret is leaked, the blast radius extends far beyond the Sigma-1 pipeline. Additionally, PATs are long-lived credentials with no automatic expiration enforcement.
findings.recommendation: Use a GitHub App installation token instead of a PAT — these are scoped to specific repositories and expire automatically after 1 hour. If a PAT must be used, create a machine user account with access only to 5dlabs/sigma-1 and use fine-grained PATs (beta) scoped to that single repo. Document the minimum required permissions (contents:write, pull_requests:write).

findings.title: No input validation on PRD intake endpoint
findings.description: Task 2 and Task 8 reference a POST /api/pipeline/intake endpoint that accepts PRD payloads, but no task addresses input validation or sanitization of the PRD content. The PRD title and description flow directly into Linear issue creation (potential injection into GraphQL mutations), Hermes research queries, GitHub PR bodies, and Discord embeds. Malicious or malformed PRD input could exploit downstream APIs.
findings.recommendation: Add an input validation subtask to Task 2: define a strict JSON schema for the PRD intake payload, validate and sanitize all string fields (max length, allowed characters, strip HTML/script tags), and reject payloads that don't conform. Use a validation library like zod or typebox in the Elysia handler.

findings.title: Discord webhook URL stored in ConfigMap instead of Secret
findings.description: Task 1 creates the DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL as a sealed secret but then references it in the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (subtask 1003 details mention 'value referencing the convention that pods mount the discord-webhook-url secret separately; set to a placeholder note'). This ambiguity could lead to the actual webhook URL being placed in the ConfigMap, which is not encrypted at rest and is readable by any pod with ConfigMap access. Discord webhook URLs are bearer credentials — anyone with the URL can post to the channel.
findings.recommendation: Ensure DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL is ONLY stored as a Kubernetes Secret, never in the ConfigMap. Remove it from the ConfigMap definition entirely. Services should mount the secret directly via secretRef or volume mount. Update subtask 1003 to explicitly exclude DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL from the ConfigMap and document that it must be consumed from the secret.

findings.title: No dependency pinning or vulnerability scanning tasks
findings.description: The plan installs npm packages (e.g., react-diff-viewer-continued v3.x in Task 4) and uses container images (curlimages/curl, busybox in Task 1) without any task for pinning exact versions, auditing dependencies for known vulnerabilities, or scanning container images. Version ranges like 'v3.x' could pull in a compromised minor/patch release.
findings.recommendation: Add a task or subtask for: (1) Pinning all npm dependency versions with a lockfile and running 'npm audit' or 'bun audit' in CI. (2) Specifying container image digests (not just tags) for all base images. (3) Integrating a container image scanner (Trivy, Grype) into the CI pipeline. (4) Add a Renovate/Dependabot configuration file for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: Raw Hermes API responses stored without access control
findings.description: Task 3 stores raw Hermes research API responses as JSON files in a deliberation artifacts directory. These may contain proprietary research content, URLs, and relevance scores. No access control, encryption, or retention policy is defined for these artifact files.
findings.recommendation: Define access controls on the artifacts directory (filesystem permissions or object storage IAM). Consider encrypting artifacts at rest. Add a retention policy (e.g., delete after 30 days). Ensure the artifacts are not served publicly via the frontend without authentication.

findings.title: Test pod with all secrets mounted creates unnecessary exposure window
findings.description: Subtask 1006 deploys a test pod that mounts ALL four secrets simultaneously and performs validation including printing secret existence checks. While the pod is deleted afterward, it creates a window where all credentials are accessible from a single attack surface, and if cleanup fails, the pod persists indefinitely.
findings.recommendation: Add a timeout/TTL on the test pod (e.g., activeDeadlineSeconds: 300). Validate secret mounting by checking existence (non-empty) without logging actual values. Consider using a Kubernetes Job instead of a Pod for automatic cleanup. Add a CI step that verifies no test pods remain after validation.

findings.title: Secret rotation CronJobs use kubectl commands inside pods requiring elevated RBAC
findings.description: Task 10 subtask 10005 implements secret rotation via CronJobs that execute 'kubectl create secret' and 'kubectl rollout restart' commands from within pods. This requires the rotator ServiceAccount to have update access to secrets and patch access to deployments, creating a privileged workload. If the CronJob pod is compromised, an attacker gains the ability to modify secrets and restart services.
findings.recommendation: Prefer External Secrets Operator with an external secret store (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) where rotation is handled by the secret provider, not by in-cluster CronJobs. If CronJobs must be used, add a PodSecurityStandard (restricted profile), run as non-root, mount a read-only filesystem, and restrict the rotator Role to only the named secrets (already partially done in 10005 but verify resourceNames constraints).

findings.title: No security event alerting or anomaly detection
findings.description: While Task 10 adds audit logging and Task 16 adds SLO-based alerting, there are no specific alerts for security events such as: repeated authentication failures, unauthorized API access attempts, secret access from unexpected pods, or unusual egress traffic patterns.
findings.recommendation: Add security-specific alert rules to Task 16: alert on repeated 401/403 responses from the PM server, alert on audit log entries showing denied RBAC requests, alert on NetworkPolicy deny logs (if the CNI supports logging denied connections). Integrate these alerts with the incident response channel.

findings.title: E2E test suite uses production credentials without isolation
findings.description: Task 8 configures CI to inject real production-grade credentials (LINEAR_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, NOUS_API_KEY) from sigma1-dev into the E2E test runner. The test suite creates real Linear issues, real GitHub PRs, and sends real API requests. There is no mention of test-specific API keys with reduced permissions or isolated test environments.
findings.recommendation: Create dedicated test-scoped API credentials with minimal permissions: a Linear API key that can only create/read issues in a test project, a GitHub token scoped to a test fork or with read-only access after PR creation, and a dedicated test Discord channel webhook. Add cleanup logic that is guaranteed to run (even on test failure) to delete test artifacts. Consider using a separate sigma1-test namespace.

findings.title: Pod security standards not enforced
findings.description: No task specifies PodSecurityStandards (PSS) or PodSecurityAdmission (PSA) configuration for the sigma1-prod or sigma1-dev namespaces. Without these, pods could run as root, mount host paths, or use privileged containers.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask to Task 10 to label the sigma1-prod namespace with 'pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted' (or at minimum 'baseline'). Ensure all Deployment specs set securityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (where possible), and drop all capabilities. Apply the same to sigma1-dev.

findings.title: Linear API token and GitHub PAT transmitted in plain text within cluster
findings.description: Services read API tokens from Kubernetes secrets and use them in outbound HTTPS calls (which is fine for external traffic), but inter-service communication within the cluster (e.g., the frontend calling the PM server) is over plain HTTP on port 3000. If the PM server proxies or returns any sensitive data including tokens in responses, they traverse the network unencrypted.
findings.recommendation: Enable mTLS between pods using a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) or at minimum configure the PM server to serve HTTPS internally with a self-signed certificate. Ensure secrets are never included in API responses to the frontend. Add a task or subtask to evaluate service mesh adoption for in-cluster encryption.

findings.title: GitHub PR creation uses broad repository write permissions
findings.description: Task 7 creates branches, commits files, and opens PRs in 5dlabs/sigma-1. The PAT needs contents:write and pull_requests:write scopes. If the PRGenerator has a bug or the PRD input is malicious, it could create arbitrary files or branches in the repository.
findings.recommendation: Validate file paths generated by the scaffold generator against an allowlist pattern (only 'tasks/{taskId}-{slug}/README.md' and 'tasks/SUMMARY.md'). Add a branch name validation to ensure only 'pipeline/{runId}' pattern is used. Consider using a GitHub App with repository-scoped permissions and branch protection rules that prevent force-pushes to main.

strengths: Network policies are planned from the start (Task 1 subtask 1005 and Task 10 subtask 10007), including default-deny egress — this is excellent defense-in-depth.
Sealed Secrets are used for credential management rather than plain Kubernetes secrets, providing encryption at rest for sensitive values.
Secret rotation is explicitly planned with defined schedules (90/180 days) and automated CronJobs with rolling restarts — many teams neglect this entirely.
Least-privilege RBAC is well-designed with dedicated ServiceAccounts per workload, namespace-scoped Roles with resourceNames constraints, and explicit prohibition of the default ServiceAccount.
Audit logging is planned with RequestResponse-level detail for mutations and authentication failures, providing a strong foundation for incident investigation.

The plan includes PodDisruptionBudgets and anti-affinity rules, which while primarily availability features, also improve security posture by ensuring no single node compromise takes down the entire service.

The DiscordNotifier and all external API clients include graceful degradation — errors are logged but don't crash the pipeline, reducing the risk of denial-of-service through downstream API manipulation.
summary: The plan demonstrates strong infrastructure security foundations including network policies, RBAC, sealed secrets, and audit logging. However, it has a critical gap: no API authentication is defined for the PM server endpoints, meaning the pipeline's intake, issue data, and PR metadata are accessible to any network-reachable client. Additionally, the use of a broad-scope GitHub PAT, lack of dependency vulnerability scanning, and missing input validation on the PRD intake represent high-to-medium severity risks that should be addressed before production deployment. Adding 3-4 targeted subtasks for API auth middleware, input validation, dependency scanning, and pod security standards would significantly reduce the overall risk profile.
